title: Non-Orthodox Nouns (Ghayr Munsarif) and Proper Nouns in Arabic
summary: This session focuses on non-orthodox nouns (ghayr munsarif) that do not take tanween or kasrah under specific conditions. The teacher explains various categories of such nouns, including female proper names, certain patterns like fa'lan and af'adu, and non-Arabic proper nouns. Examples include names like Aisha, Usama, and London.
key_points: Nouns/adjectives not taking tanween (غیر منصرƒ): Includes those with 'al-' (ال), mudaf (مudاف), or preceded by 'ya' (يا).
Female proper nouns do not take tanween (e.g., مريم, Aisha) and are inherently ghayr munsarif.
Words on specific patterns like fa'lan (e.g., قاصلان) or af'adu (أھمﺪ) are non-orthodox.
Non-Arabic proper nouns (e.g., لندن, William) do not take tanween but follow Arabic grammatical structures for case endings.
questions: [00:17:04] Q: Why does Masjidu have a dhamma instead of kasra? — A: It's on the fa'lan pattern (Masajidu).
[00:36:00] Q: How to use 'hati' for commands? — A: Singular command, e.g., 'hati' vs. plural 'hato'.
